title: Unit 30 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Compound Words & Prefixation

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 42: Compound Words. Modern Greek is like a Lego set. It loves taking two or three words and snapping them together to create something entirely new. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... is it true that a Greek word can be literally as long as a sentence?

Γεια σας! Historically, yes! Ancient Greek had some famous long words. Today, we keep it a bit more practical, but compounding is still our favorite way to create new meanings. We call them 'Sýn-the-tes lé-kse-is'—put-together words.
'Put-together words'. Let's look at the mechanics. When we combine two words, we often use a 'connecting vowel'. Usually an 'o'. Athina, show us with 'Sea' and 'Wolf'.

Take 'Thá-las-sa' (sea) and 'Lý-kos' (wolf). We drop the feminine ending of sea, add an 'o', and we get: 'Tha-las-só-ly-kos'. An old sea dog, or a sea wolf! Notice the stress moved to the connecting vowel.
That’s a key point. The stress often shifts in compound words to keep the rhythm. What about describing people? Like a 'Red-head'?
'Kók-ki-no' (red) + 'Ma-lyá' (hair). We get: 'Kok-ki-no-mál-lees'. A red-headed man. Or 'Ga-la-no-má-tees'... a blue-eyed person. 'Ga-la-nó' (light blue) + 'Má-ti' (eye).
It’s very descriptive. There are also many common prefixes that we use every day. 'Syn-', 'Poly-', 'Gyro-'.

Yes! 'Syn-' means together. 'Syn-er-ghá-zo-mai'... I work together, I cooperate. 'Po-lý-' means many. 'Po-ly-ka-toi-kée-a'... the apartment blocks you see everywhere in Athens. Literally, 'many-living' places.
And 'Gy-ro-' means around. Like 'Gy-ro-sko-pee-o' for gyroscope, or 'Gy-ró-phi-lou'... around the world? Athina, what about the most famous Greek food?
'Gy-ros'! It means 'turn' or 'circle'. When the meat turns on the spit, it’s doing a 'gý-ros'. It’s not a compound in itself, but it’s the root of so many! 'Gy-rée-zō'... I return.

Pro Tip: If you see a long word that looks scary, try to 'snap' it into two pieces. Usually, the first half is a description and the second half is the core noun. Athina, are there any 'rebel' compound words?
Some compounds keep their two stresses! Like 'Pa-i-dó-po-lis'. But mostly, they merge into one single rhythmic unit. It’s what gives Greek its 'flow'.
It’s a language of construction. This concludes Unit 42. You now have the blueprints to build your own Greek vocabulary. Excellent work.
Μπράβο σας! Συνεχίζουμε δυνατά. Γεια σας!
Excellent work. We’ll see you in Unit 43.
